justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus BR-7 as a clarifying sub-rule). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock BR-6) correctly marked invariant: true. Double-booking BR-2 marked invariant: true. Role-based cancellation correctly captured with BR-3 (patient, invariant: false for the time threshold) and BR-4 (admin-only after window, invariant: true). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the minimum of 4. Confidence level of 72 falls within the 65-90 range with clearly stated assumptions about license validation mechanism, boundary inclusivity, and half-open interval semantics.

observations.text: All 6 core rules identified with correct invariant classifications — no misclassifications

Excellent distinction between regulatory invariants and operational policies (BR-3 cancellation window correctly marked invariant: false with clear justification that the 2-hour threshold is tunable policy)

12 acceptance criteria covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-3), double-booking (AC-2), late cancellation (AC-5), admin cancellation (AC-6), unauthorized record access (AC-8), plus additional edge cases
Role boundary in cancellation thoroughly captured — separate rules for patient (BR-3) and Staff Administrator (BR-4) with distinct invariant classifications
Confidence level of 72 is well-calibrated with specific assumptions stated about license validation meaning, boundary inclusivity, and half-open interval semantics
Rich edge cases section identifying license revocation post-booking, back-to-back scheduling semantics, cancelled appointment notes, and locked note amendments
Escalations are actionable with concrete options and recommendations — particularly strong on the amendment workflow gap
BR-7 adds valuable precision about the lock timer reference point (appointment end time vs note creation time), showing deep domain understanding
Ubiquitous language section is thorough and precise, defining terms like 'Licensed Physician' and 'Note Lock' with domain-appropriate semantics
